comparison_method: intersect Paper 3 holdout IDs with Paper 2 per-id refusal judgments
experiment: LR sweep on Qwen and Llama at LR in {1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5}

shared_hyperparams.rebalance: down-sample help-side (overref) to match refuse-side (core_s2 + xl)

headline_finding: All three anchors (Qwen 2.5-3B, Llama 3.2-3B, Gemma 3-4B) train to in-distribution convergence on the same 176-200 preference pairs at appropriate LR. Training harder makes OOD held-out safety worse. Cross-lingual lift never exceeds +3.5 pp on any (anchor, LR) combination. Toxicity regresses by up to 17 pp at the most aggressive LR. The trained-but-flat-OOD signature observed on Gemma at LR=5e-6 generalises to all three anchors when LR is set appropriately, and converts to trained-and-actively-degraded-OOD as in-distribution accuracy approaches 1.0.
implications: The data scale (~176 pairs) is the bottleneck, not the optimisation. RD-DPO at this budget cannot lift OOD safety on three independent small anchor families.
There is a monotonic relationship between in-distribution learning strength and OOD toxicity regression on Qwen.
Cross-lingual delta on n=86 is inside noise (Wilson 95% CI roughly +/- 10 pp at p=0.33) for every (anchor, LR) cell -- the cross-lingual gap does not move regardless of how hard we train.
Llama jailbreak refusal stays at exactly 25% (base rate) across all three LRs. The trained adapters converge to base behaviour on jailbreak holdout despite reaching 100% in-distribution accuracy.
